17: vayeman YHWH dag gadol livlo et Yonah vayehi Yonah bime'ei haddag shlosha yamim ushlosha leilot: 'And the LORD appointed a great fish to swallow up Jonah. And Jonah was in the belly of the fish three days and three nights.' The historicity of Jonah has been debated throughout church history — a man surviving inside a large sea creature is extraordinary. Jesus's citation of Jonah in Matthew 12:40 takes the narrative as historical: 'just as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so will the Son of Man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth.' If Jonah is a parable, Jesus is drawing a parable-to-reality comparison, which is unusual. The orthodox reading: both Jonah's experience and Jesus's resurrection are historical events in which the later was the greater antitype of the earlier.
1: vayehi davar YHWH el Yonah ben Amittai lemor: 'Now the word of the LORD came to Jonah son of Amittai, saying.' The standard prophetic commissioning formula (davar YHWH) opens a book that is, uniquely, a narrative about a prophet rather than a collection of his oracles. Jonah (yonah, H3124) means 'dove' — an ironic name for a prophet who flies away rather than descending with the divine message. Ben Amittai identifies him with the historical figure of 2 Kings 14:25 (a prophet under Jeroboam II), grounding this narrative in concrete history.
2: qum lech el Nineveh hair hagedolah: 'Arise, go to Nineveh, that great city.' The dual imperative qum lech (arise-go) is the standard commissioning formula for a prophetic mission (cf. 1 Kings 17:9, Elijah to Zarephath — another Gentile mission). Nineveh (nineveh) was the capital of Assyria — Israel's most feared enemy. Veqera aleiha (cry out against it) uses the verb qara which covers both proclamation and calling out; the reason given: ki aleta ra'atam lepanai — 'for their evil has come up before me.' The phrase ra'ah will form an ironic wordplay with Jonah 4:1 (vayera... raah gedolah — it was evil to Jonah with great evil when God spared the 'evil' Nineveh).
3: vayaqam Yonah livroach Tarshishah mipnei YHWH: 'But Jonah rose to flee to Tarshish from the face of the LORD.' Three verbs of descent mark Jonah 1-2 as a structural unit: here Jonah goes down to Joppa (vayered Yafo), then down into the ship (vayered besefina), then down into the ship's interior (vayishkav vayiradam). Tarshish — traditionally identified with Tartessos in Spain — represents the western extreme of the known world, the maximum distance from Nineveh (in the east) and from YHWH's commission. The irony the narrator will develop: you cannot flee from the omnipresent God (cf. Psalms 139:9-10).
4: vaYHWH hetil ruach gedolah el hayam: 'But the LORD hurled a great wind into the sea.' The verb hetil (H2904, to hurl/cast) is the first of several occurrences of 'casting' in Jonah 1: the sailors cast cargo (v5), they cast lots (v7), Jonah asks to be cast into the sea (v12), they cast him (v15). God's sovereign hurling sets the chain in motion. Sa'ar gadol (great tempest) — the storm is YHWH's agent, not a random weather event; the ship (ha-oniyah) is described as thinking it would be broken (chashevah lehishaver), the verb chashav (to think/plan) usually applied to persons — a brief personification of the crisis.
5: vayira'u hamaluachim: 'And the sailors feared.' Three actions follow: they feared, they cried each man to his god (kol ish el elohav — the sailors are pagans worshipping multiple deities), and they cast cargo into the sea to lighten it. Meanwhile: veYonah yarad el yereketi hasefinah vayishkav vayiradam — 'Jonah had gone down into the innermost parts of the ship and was lying there fast asleep.' The descent-verb yarad again; yereketi (sides/extremities — the deep inner hold) intensifies the going-down motif. Jonah's sleep during the storm contrasts with Jesus sleeping during the storm in Matthew 8:24 — the same scene, opposite meanings: Jonah sleeps in guilty flight, Jesus sleeps in sovereign peace.
6: ma lecha nirdam: 'What are you doing here, sleeping?' The captain's rebuke (rab hachovlim = the chief of the rope-pullers/officers) is theologically charged: qum qera el eloheicha — 'arise, call on your God.' The pagan captain uses the very commissioning language YHWH had spoken to Jonah: qum qera. The captain's inadvertent echo of God's command exposes the irony: a pagan commander is telling the Hebrew prophet to do what God already told him to do. Ulai yitashtet ha'elohim lanu — 'perhaps God will think of us' — the hopeful uncertainty of the pagans contrasts with Jonah's certain knowledge of YHWH.
7: lechu venappilah goralot: 'Come, let us cast lots.' The sailors use the lot (goral, H1486) to identify the source of the calamity — standard ancient Near Eastern practice for determining divine causation. Vayipol hagoral al Yonah — 'and the lot fell on Jonah.' The sovereignty of God operates through what appears to be chance; Proverbs 16:33 states that 'the lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD.' The lot is YHWH's chosen instrument to expose his fleeing prophet — even in the mechanism of pagan divination, YHWH's hand is at work.
8: haggida lanu bishelo hara'ah hazot lanu: 'Tell us on whose account this evil has come upon us.' The sailors interrogate Jonah with four parallel questions: What is your occupation? Where do you come from? What is your country? Of what people are you? The accumulation of identity questions drives toward the point: who are you in relation to the deity who controls this storm? Bishelo — 'on account of whom' — is an unusual construction marking the causal guilt. Jonah must face what he has been running from: his identity as a servant of YHWH.
9: ivri anochi ve'et YHWH elohei hashamayim ani yare: 'I am a Hebrew, and I fear the LORD, the God of heaven.' Jonah's confession is loaded: ivri (Hebrew) identifies him with Israel's covenant identity; yerei YHWH (I fear YHWH) is the language of true piety — but used by a man who is in active flight from YHWH. The title elohei hashamayim (God of heaven) — emphasizing cosmic sovereignty — is precisely the wrong thing to tell the sailors if you want to calm them: this God made the sea and the dry land, so the storm is no accident. The sailors' fear (vayiru) intensifies.
10: ki mipnei YHWH hu voreach: 'For he was fleeing from the presence of the LORD.' The narrator's explanatory aside confirms what the sailors now understand: this man's flight from YHWH's presence is the cause of the storm. Mipnei YHWH (from the face/presence of YHWH) recalls v3 — the same phrase used three times in the chapter (vv3, 3, 10). The idiom conveys both literal distance and the covenantal relationship implied: one who stands before a king is in his service; to flee from his face is to abandon that service.
11: mah naaseh lach veyishtok hayam me'aleinu: 'What shall we do to you, that the sea may quiet down for us?' The sailors turn to Jonah for the solution — a remarkable reversal: the pagans look to the Hebrew prophet for theological guidance. Yishtoq (be quiet/calm, from shataqa = to be still) — the same verbal root used in Psalms 107:29 ('he made the storm be still [yashtoq]') — the sailors' question is answered by Jonah and then by YHWH, in sequence. The sea was going and growing more stormy (holech vesaoer) — the participle construction intensifying the worsening storm.
12: qachuni vehashlichuni el hayam veyishtok hayam me'aleichem: 'Pick me up and hurl me into the sea; then the sea will quiet down for you.' Jonah's instruction is the decisive turning point of Jonah 1. He does not volunteer to jump — the verb hashlichuni (throw me) requires the sailors' action. He accepts responsibility: ki yodea anochi ki veshelli hasaar hagadol hazeh aleichem — 'for I know that it is because of me that this great storm has come upon you.' This is Jonah's first honest speech: the acknowledgment of guilt and the substitutionary offer — throw me into the sea so you will be saved. Theologically this is the structure (if not the intent) of substitutionary atonement.
13: vayachteru ha'anashim lehashiv el hayabasha: 'Nevertheless, the men rowed hard to get back to dry land.' The verb vayachteru (from chatr = to dig; extended to rowing = digging/cutting through water) occurs only here and Psalms 139:8 (where im ashkiv sheol = if I make my bed in Sheol) — an interesting shared semantic field with the underworld theme. The sailors' effort to save Jonah without throwing him overboard demonstrates their moral instinct against bloodshed — but the sea (holech vesaer, going and raging) overwhelms their effort.
14: al titen aleinu dam naki: 'Do not put innocent blood on us.' The sailors pray to YHWH for the first time — a significant conversion-echo — using the legal formula of dam naki (innocent blood, H1818 + H5355). Their prayer acknowledges YHWH's sovereign will: ki atta YHWH kasher chafatzta asita — 'for you, O LORD, have done as it pleased you.' This is a theological confession of divine sovereignty — remarkable from pagan sailors. The fear of incurring bloodguilt for killing Jonah shows a moral seriousness that contrasts with Jonah's callousness toward the Ninevites.
15: vayise'u et Yonah vayashlichuhu el hayam vayaamod hayam mizzaopo: 'So they picked up Jonah and hurled him into the sea, and the sea ceased from its raging.' The verb vayashlichuhu (and they hurled him) completes the chain of casting begun in v4 (hetil, God hurled the wind). God's original act — hurling the storm — is answered by the sailors' act — hurling Jonah — and the sea's immediate ceasing (vayaamod, stood still) confirms the divine connection. The sea's instant response is the narrative's vindication: the sailors acted correctly, Jonah's word was true, and YHWH's purpose is accomplished through the very storm he sent.
16: vayiru ha'anashim yir'ah gedolah et YHWH: 'Then the men feared the LORD exceedingly.' The sailors' response to the calming of the sea is a complete religious transformation: vayir'u... yir'ah gedolah (feared... great fear) — the same phrase used in v5 (vayira'u hamaluachim), but now directed at YHWH rather than the storm. They offer a sacrifice (vayizbechu-zebach) and make vows (vayidderu nedarim) — the language of covenant worship. The Gentile sailors have been converted by the storm; by contrast, the Hebrew prophet required a fish.
